stem: A differentiable, invertible function \( f \) satisfies \( f([[a]])=[[b]] \) and \( f'([[a]])=[[c]] \). Find \( (f^{-1})'([[b]]) \).
solution: By the inverse function derivative rule, \( (f^{-1})'([[b]]) = 1/f'([[a]]) = 1/[[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( f'(a) \) directly instead of its reciprocal.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This mistakenly takes the reciprocal of \( a \) instead of \( f'(a) \).
This confuses \( a/f'(a) \) with the correct reciprocal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A differentiable, invertible function \( f \) satisfies \( f([[a]])=[[b]] \) and \( (f^{-1})'([[b]])=\dfrac{1}{[[k]]} \). Find \( f'([[a]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( (f^{-1})'(b) \) itself instead of inverting it to get \( f'(a) \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This adds \( a \) to the reciprocal instead of just inverting.
This divides \( a \) by \( k \) rather than inverting the given slope.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The graph of a differentiable, invertible function \( f \) passes through \( ([[a]],[[b]]) \), and the tangent line to \( y=f(x) \) there has slope \( [[m]] \). What is the slope of the tangent line to \( y=f^{-1}(x) \) at the point \( ([[b]],[[a]]) \)?
solution: The graphs of \( f \) and \( f^{-1} \) are reflections across \( y=x \), swapping the point to \( ([[b]],[[a]]) \) and inverting the slope: \( (f^{-1})'([[b]]) = 1/[[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the original slope without inverting it.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This negates the original slope instead of taking its reciprocal.
This confuses the coordinates of the point with the slope.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
